pages.full_text:
of the edge of the world. We couldn't find it. We dropped out of the cloud down to Newlyn where the art gallery is being refurbished for another week, & on into __Penzance__ where we finally found a gallery, had a bad cup of coffee & bought cakes at a frigid, Snooty cake-shop. Lid words— def a no-no.

[glued-in newspaper/printed clipping:]

[Marginalia to right of clipping, with small sketches of a curved bucket/bowl shape and a cart:]
Richard Trevithick
designed a bucket
for mining this
shape, [sketch of curved bucket shape] because
the tunnels were
this shape [sketch of oval/lozenge shape]
in the mines - it
cost less to mine
away less granite
at foot level.

[sketch of cart with wheels]
cart with
wheels, never
one end to go outside
earth.
pages.notes: Top of page is a mid-sentence continuation from previous page. A newspaper/printed clipping headed 'THE CORNISH LANGUAGE' is glued onto the page — it is a two-column vocabulary list of Cornish place-name elements and their English meanings. To the right of the clipping are handwritten marginalia with small sketches about Richard Trevithick designing a bucket shape for mining because tunnel cross-sections were oval/lozenge-shaped, and a sketch of a cart with wheels. The word 'Penzance' is underlined in the top narrative text. 'Lid words' may be a family expression. Handwritten page number not clearly visible in top-left corner. Some marginal numbers ('2', '18') visible on far right edge but their meaning is unclear — possibly bleed-through from adjacent page.

pages.full_text: We arrived at the Geevor tin mine & went underground at Zennot. We were taken round by 2 retired tin miners. 3 hours. It was fascinat[ing]. When one of them started in 1960, he was paid [by] the square fathom. Out of this money he had to pay to have his drills sharpened. The original hats were varnished felt. Until 1980s there was little awareness of safety, esp. in ventilation. 2 men banged a drill in - one held the drill, one turned it - 8 turns to a circle, until 1880s. Then compressed air drills came in, but men breathed the dust until water was passed along the central drill 1930s. Men stood on old fan belts looped over the handles once the drill was in one foot vertically. Originally, 1690, gunpowder was used with goosefeather quills, then dynamite 1880s + safety fuses 90 seconds per yard. These are still used where there might a lightening. Only too [more?] actually made the tin. At one time in 1930s they reached too near the sea in the Levant mine & a fissure opened which they plugged, (Shipconcrete 1960s) + carried on working underneath. Arsenic smells of garlic.
pages.notes: 'Zennot' could be a misspelling or idiosyncratic spelling of 'Zennor' (a village near St Ives, Cornwall) — the Geevor Tin Mine is near Pendeen, Cornwall. The Levant Mine is also near Pendeen on the West Penwith peninsula. 'Geevor' confirmed as Geevor Tin Mine, a heritage site. 'Shipconcrete' may be 'Shipsconcrete' or a brand name. Some words in the right margin are partially cut off or illegible. 'Only too [more?] actually made the tin' — wording is unclear, possibly 'Only the [ore?] actually made the tin'. Handwritten page number not clearly visible in top-right corner.
